name: Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto
shortDescription: Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto is the moon god in Japanese mythology, born from Izanagi's right eye. He governs the night and is associated with the ebb and flow of the tides.
description: Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto, also known as Tsukuyomi, is a prominent lunar deity in Japanese Shinto mythology. Born from the right eye of Izanagi, one of the creator gods, as he purified himself after visiting Yomi, the underworld, Tsukiyomi was tasked with ruling the night. While details of his actions are relatively scarce compared to his siblings Amaterasu (sun goddess) and Susanoo (storm god), his presence is essential to the cosmic order. The 'Kojiki' and 'Nihon Shoki' are the primary sources for Tsukiyomi's mythology, though they provide somewhat differing accounts. The absence of extensive narratives surrounding Tsukiyomi has led to various interpretations and speculations about his role and significance within the Shinto pantheon. Some scholars suggest that his reserved nature reflects the quiet and contemplative aspect of the moon itself.
subtitle: Ruler of the Night and Tides
role: Ruler of the night and associated with the moon's cycles.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: According to the Kojiki, Tsukiyomi-no-Mikoto was born from Izanagi's right eye as he purified himself after his descent into Yomi, the land of the dead. He was then tasked with ruling the night sky. A key myth involving Tsukiyomi tells of his visit to Ukemochi-no-Kami, the goddess of food. Disgusted by Ukemochi's method of creating food (producing it from her nose, mouth, and posterior), Tsukiyomi killed her. This act angered Amaterasu, who refused to ever face Tsukiyomi again, resulting in the separation of day and night. The Nihon Shoki presents slightly different versions of these events, sometimes omitting the gruesome details or attributing them to other deities.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Tsukiyomi's role is primarily associated with the moon and the night. His presence is crucial for maintaining the balance of the cosmos, complementing Amaterasu's reign over the day. The lack of extensive myths about Tsukiyomi has led to various interpretations of his significance. Some scholars believe he represents a more introspective and passive aspect of the divine, contrasting with the active and dynamic nature of Amaterasu and Susanoo. He is also associated with the ebb and flow of the tides, further linking him to the natural rhythms of the world. Shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi are less common than those dedicated to Amaterasu or Susanoo, but they can be found throughout Japan, often located in places with strong connections to the moon or the sea.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Tsukiyomi's symbolism is directly linked to the moon, which represents cycles, change, and the passage of time. The moon's waxing and waning phases are often interpreted as symbols of life, death, and rebirth. In contrast to the sun's association with activity and outward expression, the moon represents introspection, intuition, and the subconscious. Tsukiyomi's act of killing Ukemochi is often interpreted as a necessary but regrettable act that highlights the complexities of the divine realm. It also symbolizes the tension between purity and impurity, and the challenges of maintaining balance in the world. His association with the tides further reinforces his connection to the cyclical nature of existence.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: While not as prominent as Amaterasu or Susanoo in modern Japanese culture, Tsukiyomi continues to be recognized as an important deity in Shinto. Shrines dedicated to him still exist and are visited by those seeking blessings related to the moon, tides, or personal introspection. Tsukiyomi also appears in various forms of Japanese popular culture, including anime, manga, and video games, often depicted as a mysterious and powerful figure. His story serves as a reminder of the importance of balance and the cyclical nature of life, as well as the complexities of the divine realm. The relative scarcity of detailed narratives about Tsukiyomi allows for continued reinterpretation and reimagining of his character in contemporary contexts.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: One of the earliest written records of Japanese mythology, providing a foundational account of Tsukiyomi's birth and role.

sources.author: Anonymous
sources.description: Another early chronicle of Japanese history and mythology, offering a slightly different perspective on Tsukiyomi's origins and actions.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Associated with the tides, which are influenced by the moon.

associations.name: Night
associations.significance: Tsukiyomi is the ruler of the night.

associations.name: Cycles
associations.significance: The moon's phases represent the cyclical nature of life.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Selene/Luna
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are lunar deities associated with the moon and night.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Chandra
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A lunar deity and one of the Navagraha (nine planets) in Hinduism.

companions.name: Amaterasu
companions.description: The sun goddess and Tsukiyomi's sister, born from Izanagi's left eye. Their separation created day and night.

companions.name: Susanoo
companions.description: The storm god and Tsukiyomi's brother, born from Izanagi's nose. A more volatile and active deity.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to the Moon
worshipPractices.description: Traditional offerings of rice and sake are sometimes made to the moon to honor Tsukiyomi.

festivals.name: Tsukimi (Moon Viewing Festival)
festivals.description: A traditional Japanese festival celebrating the beauty of the moon, often associated with offerings of pampas grass and rice dumplings.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tsukiyomi appears in modern media, including anime, manga, and video games. Shrines dedicated to him still exist, fostering continued reverence.
